title: AVERT National Research Symposium 2026
theme: Youth and Extremism: Thresholds, Interventions, Connections
location: Macquarie University City Campus, Sydney, Australia
description:
The 2026 AVERT National Research Symposium is an invitation-only in-person event taking place on 26–27 November 2026 at Macquarie University's City Campus (Level 24/123 Pitt St), Sydney, Australia.

This year, we are offering a nationally focused symposium that allows a deep dive into issues of national and strategic priority for the CVE (countering violent extremism) and CT (counterterrorism) research and practitioner communities in Australia.

Bringing together researchers, practitioners, analysts and policymakers from across Australia, this year's National Symposium will explore pressing and unresolved challenges in understanding and addressing youth radicalisation, online influences and resources; challenges in youth engagement with violent extremist narratives and subcultures, and the growing involvement of young people in diverse expressions of extremist intent and violence.

Overseas presenters can register to present virtually. Australian presenters may also be able to present online in very limited circumstances, at the discretion of, and with approval from, the Symposium Organising Committee.

keynotes.name: Dr Ghayda Hassan
keynotes.institution: Professor of Clinical Psychology, Université du Québec à Montréal (UQÀM); Director & Founder, CPN-PREV
keynotes.bio: Dr Ghayda Hassan is a clinical psychologist and professor of clinical psychology at UQÀM. She has several research, clinical, and community-based national and international affiliations. She is the director of the Canada Practitioners Network for the Prevention of Extremist Violence (CPN-PREV, funded by Public Safety Canada). She is also a UNESCO co-chair on Prevention of Violence Radicalisation, as well as a researcher and clinical consultant at the SHERPA-RAPS team and the CIUSSS-COIM. She is a researcher, clinician, and policy consultant in matters of intervention in the context of violence (radicalisation, family violence, and war). Her systematic reviews, research, and clinical activities are centred around four main areas of clinical cultural psychology: social suffering, inter-community relations, radicalisation, and extremist violence; intervention in family violence and cultural diversity; identity, belonging, and mental health of children and adolescents from ethnic/religious minorities; and working with vulnerable immigrants and refugees.

themes: Radicalisation pathways, risk factors, and threshold indicators for violent extremism among young people
Assessment, intervention, diversion, and treatment approaches in youth CVE contexts
The role of parents/guardians/caretakers for young people in either prevention and intervention approaches and frameworks or in cross-generational radicalisation pathways
Issues in assessing risk and threat related to youth radicalisation (including various risk and threat assessment tools and measures)
The role of online environments, peer networks, and family dynamics in youth radicalisation
Psychologies of youth violent extremism, including mental health and neurodiversity
Gendered dimensions of youth violent extremism, including misogyny and the manosphere
Demographic and criminogenic risk factors (including gender, age, upbringing, socio-economic status)
Education system responses and primary prevention strategies
Non-extremist criminal justice intersections with youth violent extremism
Practitioner and policymaker perspectives, including de-identified case studies
encouraged: Offer innovative perspectives and conceptual approaches to youth radicalisation
Consider the perspectives and voices of young people and their experiences navigating violent extremist propaganda and environments
Where possible, draw on empirical or primary de-identified data

categories.name: Individual Papers
categories.description: Traditional academic papers may be for a maximum of 20 minutes, not including question time. Please include the name and title of the lead presenter and any co-presenters along with a 300-word maximum abstract.

categories.name: Themed Presentation Panels
categories.description: Panels consist of a maximum of 3 papers of up to 15 minutes each, not including question time, that address a common question or research/policy area. Please provide the title of the panel, details of the panel moderator and panel presenters (name, title, affiliation and paper abstracts), a 200-word maximum abstract for the panel overview, and individual panel paper abstracts (200-word maximum). Panels generally have up to 4 participants (including the panel chair). We encourage panels that combine academic and practitioner contributors.

categories.name: Roundtables and Discussion Panels
categories.description: Roundtable and Discussion panels allow for shorter presentations (maximum 5–7 minutes each) and curated discussions led by a chair (approximately 25 minutes in total), not including audience question time. Please provide the title of the roundtable/discussion panel, details of the panel moderator and panel presenters (name, title, affiliation), along with a 200-word maximum abstract. Roundtables and Discussion Panels can have a maximum of 4 participants (including the chair) — this format may be best suited for policy, community, or industry stakeholders.

categories.name: Interactive Sessions or Workshops
categories.description: Workshops can be a maximum of 45 minutes. They are meant to be interactive and led by a moderator, and must include hands-on exercises or interactions between moderators and participants. Please provide a 200-word maximum abstract, presentation title, and details of the moderator (name, title, affiliation), and indicate how the session will be conducted and what interactive techniques will be used.

requirements: Title and abstract (refer to word limits and requirements for each category above)
Names, titles and institutional affiliations of presenters/participants/moderator (for paper presentations, please include any co-authors while indicating the presenter)
A 150-word maximum bio for each presenter/participant/moderator
Contact email and telephone number for each presenter/participant/moderator
Social media handles of presenter/participant/moderator (where possible/available)

title: AVERT International Research Symposium 2025
theme: Signal or Noise? Navigating the Changing Nature of Violent Extremism Online
description:
The pace and scale of technological development is expanding the ways propaganda is disseminated and the types of people and groups who are vulnerable to violent extremism. At the same time, emergent online violent extremist subcultures and movements are becoming ever more cross-platform, transnational, and normalised. These shifting dynamics make it harder to distinguish meaningful threats (‘signal’) from background activity (‘noise’) online, necessitating better methods for threat assessment, prevention, and effective interventions.

This year’s symposium was delivered in collaboration with the Vox-Pol Network of Excellence and the Terrorism and Social Media (TASM) Conference.

keynotes.name: Professor Cynthia Miller-Idriss
keynotes.bio: Dr. Cynthia Miller-Idriss is a Professor in the School of Public Affairs and the School of Education at the American University in Washington, DC, and founding director of the Polarization and Extremism Research and Innovation Lab (PERIL).

title: AVERT International Research Symposium 2024
theme: People, Places and Spaces: New Dynamics and Shifting Responses to Violent Extremism
description: In recent years there have been demographic shifts in the people drawn to or participating in violent extremism, the places they come from and the spaces where they participate in and mobilise to violence. The age range of those involved has expanded to include both younger and older cohorts. Violent extremist networks are emerging in rural and semi-rural locales in addition to urban areas. There has also been significant growth in virtual spaces where violent extremism manifests, blurring the dichotomy between online and offline spaces.

keynotes.name: Professor Noémie Bouhana
keynotes.bio: Noémie Bouhana is Professor of Crime Science and Counter Extremism at University College London, co-leading the Counter-Terrorism Research Group. Her work focuses on the emergence of extremist social ecologies and individual vulnerability to extremism. She has directed major EU and US DoD Minerva Initiative research projects.

keynotes.name: Dr Ghayda Hassan
keynotes.bio: Dr Ghayda Hassan is a clinical psychologist and professor at UQAM, and director of the Canadian Practitioner Network for the Prevention of Radical Violence. She founded several intervention programs for individuals and families affected by violent radicalisation.

title: AVERT International Research Symposium 2023
theme: Democracy, Dissent and Countering Violent Extremism
description: The counterterrorism and countering violent extremism frameworks of many democracies were established in the immediate post-9/11 context. Today, democracies face additional challenges: increasing polarisation, declining trust in institutions, and growing adherence to anti-government conspiracies. The 2023 symposium engaged with the latest research on the role of P/CVE policies and programs in responding to these evolving dynamics.

keynotes.name: Professor Donatella della Porta
keynotes.bio: Donatella della Porta is Professor of Political Science, Dean of the Faculty of Political and Social Sciences, and Director of the PhD program at the Scuola Normale Superiore in Florence. She leads the Center on Social Movement Studies and researches social movements, political violence, and terrorism.

title: AVERT Research Symposium 2022
theme: The Space Between: New Directions for CVE Interventions
description: Interventions in countering violent extremism (CVE) have traditionally focused on three dimensions: building resilience in general society, diverting individuals at risk, and disengaging those already involved in violent extremism. The 2022 symposium brought together researchers, practitioners and policymakers to engage with the latest research on the conceptual and practical dimensions of CVE intervention.

keynotes.name: Professor John Horgan
keynotes.bio: John Horgan is a Distinguished University Professor at Georgia State University and director of the Violent Extremism Research Group (VERG). He is one of the world's leading experts on terrorist psychology, with books including The Psychology of Terrorism and Walking Away from Terrorism.

title: AVERT 2021 Research Symposium
theme: Violent Extremism at the Crossroads: Persistence, Change and Dynamism 20 Years After 9/11
description: Through generous sponsorship from the Australian Department of Home Affairs, AVERT hosted over 200 online participants from around the world for three days of intellectual and practical inquiry on the contemporary dynamics of violent extremism 20 years after 9/11. The symposium featured keynote addresses from Professor Martha Crenshaw and Professor Maura Conway.

keynotes.name: Professor Maura Conway
keynotes.bio: Maura Conway is Paddy Moriarty Professor of Government and International Studies at Dublin City University, Professor of Cyber Threats at Swansea University, and Coordinator of VOX-Pol.

keynotes.name: Professor Martha Crenshaw
keynotes.bio: Martha Crenshaw is senior fellow emerita at the Center for International Security and Cooperation, Stanford University, and professor emerita of Government at Wesleyan University. She was a Guggenheim Fellow and a Corresponding Fellow of the British Academy.
